name: Bhartrprapanca
dates_notes: Known only via Śaṅkara's polemic in the Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Bhāṣya; no surviving work; reconstruction by Hiriyanna 1924 from citation-fragments.

lineage_polemical.note: Śaṅkara's Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Bhāṣya is the principal source for and refutation of Bhartṛprapañca's bhedābheda.

core_thesis: Bhartṛprapañca, as reconstructed from later citations (principally Hiriyanna 1924, drawing on Śaṅkara's Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Bhāṣya, Sureśvara's Vārttika, and Ānandagiri's gloss), holds a *bhedābheda* (difference-and-non-difference) Vedānta in which *brahman* is intrinsically both one and many: the Upaniṣadic *neti neti* and the affirmative cosmogonic passages are together literal, not concessive. *Brahman* genuinely transforms (*pariṇāma*) into the world of names-and-forms (*nāma-rūpa*) while remaining the unitary substrate; the *jīva* is a real *aṃśa* of *brahman*, and *karma* combined with *jñāna* (the *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* doctrine) is the operative *sādhana* for liberation. *Mukti* is realization of one's identity as a real differentiated mode of *brahman*, not the cancellation of phenomenal multiplicity. What is firmly attested across the citation-fragments: (a) the dual-aspect ontology, (b) *jñāna-karma-samuccaya*, (c) the eight-aspect (*aṣṭa-rūpa*) account of *brahman*, and (d) the *jīva*'s real partial identity with *brahman*. Everything else in the reconstruction is conjectural.

engaged_works.title: Reconstructed Brhadaranyaka commentary fragments
engaged_works.ascription_notes: No work survives; the corpus is a modern reconstruction (Hiriyanna 1924) from citations preserved in Śaṅkara, Sureśvara, and Ānandagiri.
engaged_works.summary: No work by Bhartṛprapañca survives. The corpus is a modern reconstruction — principally M. Hiriyanna, 'Bhartṛprapañca: An Old Vedāntin' (*Indian Historical Quarterly*, 1924), with later supplements — assembled from (i) Śaṅkara's Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Upaniṣad-Bhāṣya, where Bhartṛprapañca is the principal *pūrvapakṣin* across multiple chapters and is named explicitly; (ii) Sureśvara's Bṛhadāraṇyaka-Vārttika, which expands on Śaṅkara's refutations and preserves further fragments; (iii) Ānandagiri's sub-commentary, which sometimes labels otherwise-unattributed positions as Bhartṛprapañca's; and (iv) scattered references in later Vedāntic literature. The reconstruction yields a commentary that read the Bṛhadāraṇyaka as teaching *bhedābheda* with *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* — a *prima facie* plausible reading of that Upaniṣad's dual emphasis on cosmogonic affirmation (Yājñavalkya's enumerations) and apophatic limit (*neti neti*). The text-historical situation is irreducibly speculative: the cited fragments are filtered through opponents, and any 'doctrine' attributed to him is a Śaṅkaran summary, not an authorial voice.
